metadata.title: The Sociocultural Context of the Eskaya Tribe: Preserving  Heritage Amid Modern  Challenges
metadata.description:
ABSTRACT  

This study sought to describe the sociocultural practices of the Eskaya tribe in Taytay, Duero, Bohol, using ethnographic research and PESTEL analysis. The findings highlight key aspects of the tribe's political, economic, social, technological, environmental, and legal systems, emphasizing their cultural significance and need for preservation. The Eskaya tribe sustains itself through gardening and farming. It reflects a simple rural lifestyle intertwined with unique cultural practices, including their indigenous language, mathematical numeration, and writing system. The study underscores the critical role of education, policy, and research in safeguarding these cultural elements. Initiatives like the Mother Tongue-Based Multilingual Education (MTBMLE) program and enhanced training in the Eskayan script are pivotal in promoting cultural awareness. Policy measures are vital for protecting the tribe's ancestral domain and natural resources amid social and environmental changes that threaten traditional values. Ethnographic observations and interviews with teachers, parents, and students revealed the tribe's vibrant social practices, celebrations, and indigenous technological methods in farming. The legal framework provided by the Indigenous Peoples Rights Act (RA 8371) supports their rights and cultural heritage. Ultimately, the study advocates for increased awareness and deeper research to preserve and celebrate the Eskaya tribe's unique identity for future generations.

 

Keywords: Eskaya tribe, ethnographic research, preservation, sociocultural practices

creators.name: Janiola, Fe R.
creators.affiliation: College of Education, Holy Name University, Tagbilaran City, Bohol,Philippines

metadata.title: Biography of Nirala - A resilient spirit who freed hindi literature
metadata.description:
In the vast and vibrant landscape of Indian literature, few figures stand as boldly original and quietly revolutionary as Suryakant Tripathi ‘Nirala’. To call him merely a poet would be to confine a spirit that refused all boundaries—linguistic, cultural, artistic, and ideological. He was not only a creator of verse but a force that reshaped the very soul of modern Hindi literature.

It is therefore both timely and deeply meaningful that this volume, “Suryakant Tripathi Nirala: An Unsung Hero,” brings together a comprehensive, insightful, and passionately argued exploration of Nirala’s life, works, and enduring legacy.

 

Actually I am not from the hard-core field of literature. My area of research interest is basically animal science. Apart from this I have a great interest on Indian literature. I served 26 year as a associate professor at Mahishadal Raj College which was the birthplace of Suryakanta Tripathi Nirala. As professor of Zoology I felt Deep attraction to the work of Nirala. I went through every writing of Nirala and its creation. For a long time I felt and urge to contribute something about work of Nirala.

 

I performed interdisciplinary sensitivity—combining scientific precision with literary depth—to illuminate the many dimensions of Nirala’s genius.

 

This book is not merely a biography, nor is it just a critical study. It is a journey—through history, literature, music, philosophy, and human resilience. Beginning with an introduction to Nirala’s life and times, the work carefully unfolds his personal struggles, creative brilliance, and social consciousness. Each chapter adds a new layer, revealing a man who was as complex as he was courageous.

 

Particularly noteworthy is the breadth of coverage. From Nirala’s creative output—his poems, novels, essays, and songs—to his linguistic innovations and philosophical outlook, the book presents a panoramic view of his contributions. The chapters examining his relationship with Gandhiji, the influence of Rabindranath Tagore, and the broader context of Hindi literature before and after Nirala are especially illuminating. They position him not in isolation, but as a central figure in a dynamic cultural and intellectual movement.

 

Equally compelling is the attention given to the struggles that shaped Nirala’s life—his financial hardships, personal losses, and relentless battles against social and artistic conventions. These elements do not merely evoke sympathy; they reveal the indomitable spirit that fueled his their entire creative production and made his voice so distinct and enduring.

 

The later chapters, which delve into themes such as gender hybridity, national identity, populism, and Nirala’s role as a social reformer, reflect a modern critical perspective. They make this book not only relevant for literary scholars but also for readers interested in cultural studies, history, and the evolution of Indian thought.

 

What sets this work apart is its sincerity. It does not attempt to mythologize Nirala, but rather to understand him—his contradictions, his innovations, his humanity. In doing so, it succeeds in presenting him as he truly was: a pioneer who ‘freed’ Hindi poetry, a visionary who gave voice to the marginalized, and an artist whose relevance continues to resonate in contemporary times.

 

I think this book will serve as a valuable resource for students, researchers, and lovers of literature alike. More importantly, it will inspire readers to rediscover Nirala—not just as a literary figure, but as a symbol of courage, creativity, and uncompromising individuality.

 

In bringing this hard work to light, I performed a significant service to the world of letters. One hopes that

 

this book will spark renewed interest in Nirala’s writings and encourage deeper engagement with the rich heritage of Hindi literature.

creators.name: Dr. SUBHAMOY DAS
creators.affiliation: Associate Professor and Head of Zoology at Mahishadal Raj College Vidyasagar University

data.title: Biography of Nirala - A resilient spirit who freed hindi literature

metadata.title: The Impact of Terrorism on Indian Tourism
metadata.description:
Abstract 

This research article provides an in-depth, multi-dimensional examination of the complex relationship between terrorism and the tourism industry in India. Tourism serves as a foundational pillar of the Indian economy, contributing substantially to Gross Domestic Product (GDP), employment generation, and socio-economic development across diverse regions. However, the industry is inherently sensitive to perceptions of safety, stability, and geopolitical security. This paper investigates the immediate and long-term economic and psychological impacts of terrorist incidents on international tourist arrivals (ITA), domestic travel patterns, and revenue streams.

By synthesizing historical data, analyzing pivotal case studies such as the 2008 Mumbai attacks, evaluating the nuances of modern "digital diplomacy," and examining the role of domestic tourism as a shock absorber, this study explores the systemic resilience of the Indian tourism sector. The findings suggest that while terrorism triggers significant short-term volatility and creates complex "spillover effects" on global risk perception, the Indian market exhibits high systemic resilience. This resilience is fundamentally driven by a robust domestic tourism base, proactive crisis management, and an evolving framework of "resilient branding."

This paper ultimately argues that the future of Indian tourism depends on the sophisticated integration of advanced digital risk management, public-private partnerships (PPP), and a strategic move toward experiential and decentralized tourism models that decouple physical security incidents from destination perception. We further posit that true resilience requires shifting from a reactive posture—where policies are formed in the wake of trauma—to a proactive, integrated security-hospitality model that treats traveler safety as a core component of the "Incredible India" value proposition. This analysis aims to serve as a roadmap for stakeholders in managing the delicate balance between openness and security in an era of asymmetric threats.

Keywords: Terrorism, Indian Tourism, Economic Impact, Security Perception, Crisis Management, Resilience, Tourism Policy, Destination Image, Soft Power, Digital Diplomacy, Risk Mitigation, Economic Multiplier, Experiential Tourism

1. Introduction

The tourism industry is frequently categorized as a "peace-time industry," a designation that underscores the fundamental requirement of free, safe, and frictionless movement of people across borders. For a developing nation like India, which boasts immense cultural, historical, and geographical diversity—ranging from the high-altitude landscapes of the Himalayas to the tropical backwaters of Kerala—tourism is not merely an economic activity but a vital engine for national development and a primary instrument of soft power. It fosters infrastructure growth, provides direct and indirect employment to millions of individuals, and preserves diverse cultural heritages that might otherwise vanish in the face of rapid urbanization. Since the liberalization of the Indian economy in the early 1990s, the sector has grown exponentially, becoming a beacon for foreign investment and a cornerstone of the "Incredible India" brand, which has successfully marketed the country’s ancient heritage to a global audience.

However, India has historically navigated a complex landscape of regional security and asymmetric threats, making it an ideal case study for examining the nexus between security and travel. This research seeks to provide a rigorous, evidence-based analysis of the causal link between terrorist activities and the tourism sector. It examines not only the immediate, observable fiscal losses but also the underlying dynamics that allow the sector to sustain itself—and eventually recover—amidst persistent, localized, and sometimes nationalized security threats. By exploring the triad of security, perception, and economics, this study aims to provide a strategic roadmap for policy makers, hospitality stakeholders, and international organizations to build a more secure, inclusive, and resilient future for Indian tourism.

Beyond the immediate economic metrics, the scope of this study encompasses the social and psychological dimensions of travel security. As India seeks to position itself as a global leader in tourism, the ability to manage and mitigate the reputational damage associated with security incidents becomes a defining feature of a mature, globally competitive tourism market. This introduction sets the stage for a critical examination of how India has managed the transition from a passive, reactive posture to a sophisticated, technology-integrated, and proactive approach to tourism security. The evolution from a destination defined by its colonial history to one defined by modern dynamism necessitates a parallel evolution in how security and hospitality interact in the global public eye. The challenge is to maintain the allure of a vibrant, welcoming destination while simultaneously addressing the legitimate security concerns of international visitors, ensuring that the "Incredible India" narrative remains untarnished by the shadow of global instability.

2. Literature Review: The Theoretical Framework of Risk

Academic literature on the tourism-terrorism nexus consistently highlights that the hospitality sector is among the most vulnerable to geopolitical instability. The relationship between terror and tourism is governed by several theoretical frameworks that explain how localized events translate into global industry shocks, creating a volatile environment where perception often outweighs physical reality.

  • The Demand Side (Risk Perception Theory): Prospective international tourists, heavily influenced by global media cycles and government-issued travel advisories, often exhibit a heightened perception of risk. This leads to a rapid decline in demand for destinations associated with conflict, a phenomenon often described as the "cancellation effect." In the age of social media, this perception is amplified by the "geographic shadow" effect, where the perceived risk area extends far beyond the actual site of an incident, causing cancellations in regions that are geographically remote and objectively safe. Travelers often lack the granular geographical knowledge to distinguish between an affected urban center and a peaceful province thousands of kilometers away. This ignorance leads to a "blanket" risk assessment that disproportionately punishes unaffected regions. Cognitive biases, such as the "availability heuristic," further exacerbate this, where recent, vivid news reports override statistically objective safety data.

  • The Supply Side (Operational Cost Inflation): Increased security requirements, higher insurance premiums for transport and hospitality, and the necessity of capital-intensive investments in surveillance technology drive up the operational costs for stakeholders. These costs squeeze profit margins, forcing providers to balance competitive pricing with elevated security standards. Often, the investment in "hard" security measures like metal detectors, perimeter walls, and X-ray scanners can create an institutionalized atmosphere of fear, which may indirectly detract from the guest experience by emphasizing the threat rather than the destination's appeal. This leads to the "securitization trap," where the effort to make a place safer makes it feel less like a leisure destination and more like a high-risk zone.

  • The Crisis Management Lifecycle (CMLC): Modern scholarship suggests that destinations must navigate a cycle of mitigation, preparedness, response, and recovery. In India, the challenge has historically been a reactive, rather than a proactive, approach. However, there is a clear shift toward "proactive resilience," where stakeholders are integrating crisis communication into their standard marketing strategies to maintain brand equity even during periods of volatility. This lifecycle approach emphasizes that recovery begins long before an incident occurs, through the building of strong stakeholder relationships, regular audits, and the establishment of transparent, real-time communication channels. It involves moving from a "crisis response" mindset to an "issue management" mindset.

  • The Substitution Effect: Existing models suggest that international tourists rarely stop traveling entirely following a localized conflict; rather, they substitute their intended destination for one perceived as safer. The challenge for India, therefore, is to maintain its competitive position in the global tourism marketplace during periods of negative security narratives, ensuring that the "Incredible India" brand retains its allure as a preferred, rather than a risky, destination. By highlighting the unique experiences that cannot be replicated elsewhere, India seeks to raise the "cost of substitution," making the destination indispensable to the global traveler.

  • The Social Amplification of Risk Framework (SARF): This framework explains how psychological, social, and institutional processes in the media can amplify or attenuate perceptions of risk. In the context of tourism, an incident is rarely just a physical event; it is a "communicated event." The way the media frames the event determines the longevity of the damage to the destination's image. SARF suggests that the "social station" of the victims and the "dread factor" of the attack method significantly influence the intensity of the public’s risk response, often leading to disproportionate and long-lasting avoidance behaviors. This framework is crucial for understanding why some regions struggle for years after a single incident, while others recover in months.

  • The Asymmetric Threat Model: Unlike traditional warfare, terrorism against tourism often utilizes asymmetric tactics—targeting soft, crowded, or iconic locations to maximize visibility. This requires a rethink of security: protecting not just government assets, but the social fabric and public spaces where tourists congregate. The goal of the terrorist in these scenarios is to destroy the "sense of place" and the atmosphere of normalcy that defines the tourism experience. Asymmetric threats are inherently difficult to predict, placing the burden of security on the tourism sector itself to be constantly vigilant.

  • Securitization Theory: This emerging area of research examines how an issue is "securitized"—framed as an existential threat that requires emergency measures. In tourism, excessive securitization can paradoxically damage the industry by creating an atmosphere of tension, surveillance, and anxiety, which conflicts with the fundamental desire for leisure and exploration. Finding the balance between "visible security" and "invisible hospitality" is a critical theoretical and practical challenge, requiring a move towards sophisticated, unobtrusive security protocols that maintain traveler comfort while ensuring safety. The goal is to provide a seamless transition from the airport to the hotel, where safety measures feel like a luxury standard rather than a police-state necessity.

3. The Indian Context: Complexity and Spillover

India’s tourism landscape is uniquely complex. With its vast geographic expanse, terrorist incidents are often highly localized. However, the globalization of information ensures that even a localized event can create a "country-level" risk perception in international markets. This is particularly problematic because travel advisories issued by Western nations often generalize risks, disproportionately impacting the tourism sector even when the vast majority of the country remains entirely unaffected. The heterogeneity of India—with its mix of bustling, dense urban centers and vast, remote natural landscapes—is often lost in international media coverage, which tends to flatten the country into a monolithic entity.

The "spillover effect"—where a security incident in one state leads to booking cancellations in another region thousands of miles away—is a persistent challenge for the Indian Ministry of Tourism. For instance, an incident in a border region can lead to a measurable dip in leisure travel to coastal resorts in the south. This demonstrates that in the global mind, India is often treated as a singular, monolithic destination rather than a diverse, subcontinent-sized nation. Mitigating this requires a nuanced communication strategy that emphasizes the geographical distance between incidents and key tourism hubs, utilizing data visualization and real-time updates to counteract generalized travel warnings. The Ministry of Tourism’s ability to communicate the "geographical buffer" between conflict zones and tourism hotspots is paramount to managing international visitor anxiety.

Furthermore, India’s decentralized governance structure means that tourism infrastructure and security are often managed by different administrative bodies. Bridging the gap between state-level police forces and central tourism promotion agencies is a constant logistical hurdle, one that requires a unified policy framework to ensure that security responses do not inadvertently signal instability to the global tourism market. The federal system requires a high degree of inter-agency coordination to ensure that "security signals" (such as increased police presence or cordoned areas) are balanced against the "hospitality signals" (accessibility, ease of movement, and guest comfort).

The regional disparity in risk perception is also shaped by media bias. Urban centers like Delhi, Mumbai, and Bengaluru are frequently scrutinized, while rural and eco-tourism hubs often escape the negative attention of international press. This presents an opportunity for India to pivot its marketing towards these "low-risk, high-experience" zones, decentralizing the tourism footprint and reducing the concentration of economic risk. By promoting the relative isolation and safety of emerging tourism circuits, India can create a "layered" tourism brand that is resilient to localized urban shocks. This strategy shifts the focus from the "monolithic India" to a "multi-dimensional India," where travelers are encouraged to explore the hidden gems that exist far from the high-pressure zones of the global news cycle.

4. Assessing Economic Impact

The economic consequences of terrorist events on the tourism industry are broad, multifaceted, and ripple through various sectors of the economy. When a security event occurs, the immediate impact is a sharp decline in visitor numbers as potential travelers cancel or postpone trips. This reduction in tourist arrivals leads to a direct loss in revenue for the hospitality and travel sectors, including airlines, hotels, tour operators, and local transport providers. The loss is most acute in the immediate aftermath of an event, where the uncertainty and fear lead to a "sudden stop" in reservations, causing an immediate liquidity crunch for service providers.

Beyond this direct revenue loss, the industry experiences the "multiplier effect." Tourism is deeply integrated into the local economy; when tourists stay away, the impact is felt by small-scale businesses, including local guides, handicraft artisans, and restaurant owners. A reduction in visitor spending reduces income for these stakeholders, potentially leading to job losses and diminished local economic activity. The tourism industry is characterized by its labor-intensive nature, meaning that downturns have immediate and tangible effects on employment levels, often leading to cycles of temporary or permanent layoffs that further suppress local consumer demand. This, in turn, creates a negative feedback loop: local businesses, deprived of tourism revenue, lack the capital to maintain the quality of their offerings, further reducing the destination's appeal.

Furthermore, we must consider the long-term impact on investment. Following significant security incidents, foreign direct investment (FDI) in the hospitality sector often plateaus. Investors and hotel chains, sensitive to perceived stability, may delay or cancel planned capital-intensive projects such as new luxury resorts or infrastructure developments. This "wait-and-see" approach creates a long-term drag on capacity expansion and essential infrastructure growth, which are crucial for the industry's recovery and future expansion. FDI in tourism is a long-term commitment, and persistent concerns regarding stability can redirect this capital to other, more "predictable" global markets. This results in a "lost generation" of development, where the destination falls behind global standards for luxury, sustainability, and technological integration.

Additionally, the insurance market for tourism-related risks tightens significantly following such events. The cost of liability and travel insurance for tour operators skyrockets, which must be passed on to the consumer, potentially pricing out price-sensitive tourists. Ultimately, the total economic toll of terrorism extends well beyond the immediate loss of ticket or room revenue, encompassing a significant impact on indirect employment, the valuation of hospitality real estate, and future developmental potential for the tourism sector. This economic drag is often most severe for luxury and MICE (Meetings, Incentives, Conferences, and Exhibitions) tourism, which are disproportionately sensitive to high-level corporate security mandates. Corporate travel planners are generally more risk-averse than leisure travelers, and their withdrawal can be immediate and sustained for several years.

The industry also suffers from "operational overhead," where constant investment in security—metal detectors, enhanced staffing, private security personnel, and data-driven threat assessment tools—becomes a permanent, non-negotiable expense. While these costs ensure safety, they also shift resources away from product development, guest experience enhancements, and marketing initiatives, creating a subtle but persistent opportunity cost for the industry. This institutionalization of security is a double-edged sword: it provides necessary protection but also transforms the tourism environment into one that is defined by its security measures rather than its natural and cultural allure.

5. Comparative Case Studies: Crisis and Response

5.1. The 2008 Mumbai Attacks

The 2008 Mumbai attacks represented a watershed moment for Indian tourism and security policy. The event targeted the high-end, international segment of the market (luxury hotels, iconic landmarks, and transport hubs). The psychological impact was immense, as it shattered the notion that luxury hotels were "safe havens." The recovery was initially slow, but the event forced the tourism sector to overhaul its security protocols. By 2010, the "Incredible India" campaign was recalibrated to emphasize both the cultural warmth of India and its significantly enhanced safety measures. This crisis led to the establishment of specialized counter-terrorism hubs and a more integrated approach between private security and state law enforcement, setting a new standard for hotel security in the region.

The lessons learned here were fundamental in shifting the industry toward a "secure by design" approach for new hospitality infrastructure. It taught the industry that in the age of global media, the response to a crisis is just as important as the security measures themselves. Hotels began implementing "invisible" security measures—such as integrated biometrics and subtle, non-intrusive surveillance—that provided high levels of safety without feeling like a bunker. This evolution was critical in restoring the confidence of luxury travelers, demonstrating that the destination had evolved from its traumatic history and was now better prepared to manage modern security threats.

5.2. Regional Tensions and the "Resilience Threshold"

In regions with recurring localized security challenges, such as parts of Jammu and Kashmir, tourism dynamics operate through a different mechanism. Here, international tourism is highly sensitive to even minor security upticks, while domestic tourism (pilgrimages and leisure) often shows remarkable stability. This demonstrates a "resilience threshold": when domestic tourists continue to visit despite external or internal threats, it sends a powerful signal to the international market that the destination remains viable. This domestic demand acts as a "floor," preventing a complete collapse of the local economy during lean years and allowing infrastructure to persist until the return of international travelers. This duality between volatile international interest and steady domestic engagement is a cornerstone of India's tourism stability, showcasing the power of internal market demand to insulate the industry from global volatility.

The strategy in these regions has shifted towards "community-based resilience," where local stakeholders, guides, and hotel owners act as informal, yet highly effective, ambassadors of stability. By demonstrating their own commitment to keeping the tourism economy alive, these locals provide an authenticity that formal government marketing campaigns often struggle to replicate. This "ground-up" resilience is becoming a primary focus for India’s tourism recovery plans in conflict-prone areas.

5.3. Evolving Risk Profiles and Adaptive Governance

Unlike the 2008 attacks, more recent incidents have been met with more sophisticated digital responses. The government now utilizes rapid-response social media teams to provide verified information, preventing the circulation of rumors that historically exacerbated the "spillover effect." This evolution from reactive, silence-based crisis management to proactive, information-led communication represents a significant step forward in Indian tourism policy. It demonstrates that the battle for tourism is no longer just on the ground, but in the digital information space. Governments are now leveraging real-time data to provide assurances to foreign embassies, thereby shortening the duration of negative travel advisories.

The shift towards "transparent governance"—sharing honest, timely, and verified updates with stakeholders—has replaced the outdated approach of attempting to downplay or suppress information, which only erodes trust over the long term. This transparency has proven to be a vital asset in rebuilding credibility with international travel partners. By treating the global tourism market as an informed stakeholder rather than a passive observer, India has demonstrated that it is capable of managing security crises with the same level of professional maturity expected from any world-class destination.

6. Psychological Impacts and the Mediatization of Risk

Beyond the quantifiable economic data, the psychological impact is profound. Terrorism leverages fear to alter human behavior, and in the digital age, this process is hyper-accelerated. The "mediatization" of terror—where 24-hour news cycles, viral social media trends, and algorithmic search result optimization amplify the scope and frequency of events—shapes how potential travelers perceive a destination. The way an incident is framed—whether as an isolated criminal act or as a systemic, country-wide security failure—dictates the extent of the traveler's avoidance behavior. This "digital framing" is often detached from the ground reality, creating an "echo chamber" of fear that is exceptionally difficult to break.

In the context of India, the branding of a destination has shifted from purely aesthetic (monuments, landscapes, culture) to "safe and secure" experiences. This transition requires significant investment in Public Relations (PR) to counteract global media narratives that focus exclusively on instability. The perception of risk is not static; it is shaped by the duration and frequency of events, the type of target, and the speed and transparency of the government’s response. If a government is perceived as having control, the recovery of the "brand image" is significantly faster. Today, "Digital Diplomacy" is as critical as physical security; if the narrative online is not controlled, the negative stigma can last significantly longer than the actual physical threat.

Furthermore, we must address the "fear of the unknown." For many international tourists, an unfamiliar, distant location is already perceived as high-risk. When terrorism is added to this perception, the threshold for cancellation becomes much lower. Therefore, the goal of tourism marketing post-incident is not just to prove safety, but to replace the "fear of the unknown" with the "familiarity of the experience," utilizing testimonials and influencer campaigns that show the normality of life in the destination. The psychological strategy, therefore, is to humanize the destination, reminding the global audience that the residents of the destination are the primary victims of terror, and that their resilience is a testament to the destination's core values. This involves shifting from "top-down" official statements to "bottom-up" personal stories that highlight the warmth and hospitality of the local population, effectively countering the dehumanizing effects of fear-based media narratives. By giving a "human face" to the destination, India can bridge the psychological gap between the concerned traveler and the reality of life in the country.

7. Resilience and Recovery Strategies

Despite the multifaceted challenges, India has demonstrated remarkable resilience. Key strategies that have facilitated recovery include:

  • Diversification of Tourism Products: Shifting the focus from vulnerable urban centers to diverse experiential tourism—such as rural tourism, wellness tourism in Kerala, and adventure tourism in the Himalayas—reduces the concentration risk. By spreading the tourist footprint across the country, India ensures that a localized incident does not paralyze the entire national sector. This diversification also attracts a different type of tourist—the experiential traveler—who is often more resilient and less swayed by mainstream media panic than the traditional mass-market tourist.

  • The "Incredible India" Campaign: This initiative has been instrumental in rebranding the country post-crisis, focusing on positive narratives to "crowd out" negative security-related news. The strategy often involves engaging travel influencers and international media to highlight the "business as usual" nature of daily life in India. By flooding the digital space with high-quality, aspirational content, the campaign effectively recalibrates the user's focus from danger to discovery, creating a "narrative shield" that protects the brand from localized volatility.

  • Integrated Security Infrastructure: The integration of advanced surveillance (CCTV, facial recognition, and perimeter defense) and the establishment of dedicated Tourism Police units in high-traffic zones has improved the "felt safety" of tourists, which is often as important as actual safety. These units provide a visible, professional, and helpful presence that assures tourists of their security without feeling overly intrusive or militarized.

  • Leveraging the Domestic Base: As mentioned, aggressive marketing to the domestic middle class acts as a shock absorber. This internal demand ensures that hotels and transport systems remain operational, preserving jobs and maintaining the tourism ecosystem until international demand recovers. This base provides a constant, reliable revenue stream that reduces the industry's total dependence on volatile international visitor flows.

  • Community-Based Tourism (CBT): Empowering local communities to manage and protect their own tourism resources creates a sense of ownership and vigilance. When local communities are stakeholders, they become the first line of defense, ensuring that tourism activities are conducted sustainably and securely. This decentralization of security and management fosters a more genuine, welcoming experience that is less likely to be perceived as a state-controlled or "secured" environment.

  • Public-Private Collaboration: Encouraging hotels to share threat intelligence and training, ensuring that the entire hospitality ecosystem operates with shared standards. This collaborative model, often facilitated through industry associations, ensures that security is treated as a shared public good rather than a competitive advantage. It fosters a culture of mutual support, where hotels coordinate on evacuation protocols, security best practices, and emergency response, significantly reducing the impact of any single incident.

  • Hosting Global Mega-Events: The strategic hosting of international events—such as summits, sporting championships, or cultural festivals—serves as a high-visibility demonstration of stability. These events force a rigorous security upgrade that remains in place long after the event, serving as a legacy benefit for the tourism sector at large, and providing a powerful message of global integration and normalcy.

8. Policy Recommendations

To bolster long-term resilience, the following policy interventions are recommended:

  • Crisis Communication Protocols: Establishing a centralized, rapid-response communication wing within the Ministry of Tourism to provide real-time updates and combat misinformation during security incidents, ensuring that the global media does not over-report or inaccurately frame isolated events. This wing must be empowered to work with global news outlets and digital platforms to ensure that accurate, context-rich information is available during the critical first 24 hours of a crisis.

  • Public-Private Partnership (PPP) in Security: Encouraging private hospitality entities to invest in shared security infrastructure, incentivized by tax rebates. This would create a "security net" around tourist hubs, funded by both state and private interests. Such partnerships could also include shared training programs for security personnel, ensuring consistent protocols across the sector.

  • Predictive Analytics: Utilizing AI-driven modeling to predict demand fluctuations post-crisis, allowing for more precise, targeted marketing interventions that can deploy resources exactly where and when they are needed most to revive local sectors. This proactive approach avoids wasteful "blanket" advertising and ensures that promotional budgets are used efficiently to restore traveler confidence.

  • Travel Insurance Reform: Promoting and subsidizing specialized travel insurance products that mitigate the financial impact of cancellations due to security warnings, making it cheaper and safer for international tourists to book during perceived periods of risk. This institutionalizes risk-sharing, ensuring that the financial burden of instability is not borne entirely by the tourist or the local operator.

  • Digital Diplomacy and Sentiment Analysis: Investing in advanced sentiment analysis tools to monitor global conversations about India, allowing the government to proactively address negative narratives before they harden into long-term policy or travel advisories. This also involves working closely with international travel bloggers and media to ensure that the "on-the-ground" reality is reported accurately.

  • Capacity Building for Stakeholders: Training staff in the hospitality sector—from high-end managers to local tour guides—on crisis management and emergency response, ensuring that the "soft" infrastructure of tourism is as resilient as the "hard" infrastructure. This involves creating a culture of alertness where hospitality staff view themselves as the first line of both service and security.

  • Strategic Geographical Branding: Moving away from country-wide travel advisories toward region-specific updates, helping international governments distinguish between affected zones and safe, popular tourism hotspots. By providing granular data, India can encourage foreign governments to localize their advisories, preventing unnecessary mass-cancellations in unaffected states.

9. Discussion and Findings

The research indicates that the impact of terrorism on Indian tourism is generally short-lived, with a typical recovery cycle lasting between 6 to 18 months. The sector is characterized by high elasticity; demand tends to return once the immediate threat perception dissipates. However, the recurring nature of these threats necessitates a permanent state of readiness.

The "resilience" of Indian tourism is not accidental; it is the result of a vast, culturally rich market that provides an inherent buffer against isolated incidents. We find that the strength of the tourism sector is intrinsically linked to the strength of the state's crisis response and its ability to maintain the "Incredible India" narrative in the face of adversity. This resilience is further bolstered by the growing domestic middle class, which serves as a financial and structural floor during international demand troughs.

The findings suggest that the most successful destinations are those that prioritize "transparency-based security." Tourists are increasingly aware of global security realities; they do not expect a zero-risk environment, but they do expect the host nation to be transparent about risks and proactive in their management. By transitioning toward this model, India can transform its image from a "risky" destination to a "resilient and prepared" one. The key finding of this research is that resilience is not merely about the ability to bounce back, but the ability to evolve; it is about learning from each crisis to create a more robust, decentralized, and digitally connected tourism ecosystem.

Furthermore, the discussion highlights the emerging role of sustainability as a form of security. Destinations that prioritize community welfare and environmental stewardship are naturally more resilient to external shocks, as they have cultivated deep local support systems that act as a buffer during times of crisis. When tourism is local, the benefits are local, and the stakeholders have a vested interest in maintaining peace and order, creating a sustainable foundation for long-term growth.

Conclusion

Terrorism remains a critical challenge to the sustainable growth of Indian tourism, acting as an intermittent but significant disruptor of economic progress. While the economic impact is undeniable and often severe in the short term, the resilience of the sector is bolstered by a robust domestic tourism market and effective, proactive state intervention. Moving forward, the focus must shift toward "resilient branding"—an approach that ensures the narrative of India as a welcoming, culturally vibrant, and safe destination remains more potent than the sporadic, and often amplified, narrative of instability.

Future policy must focus on balancing necessary, non-intrusive security measures with the preservation of the open, welcoming atmosphere that defines the Indian travel experience. The ultimate goal for India’s tourism policy should be the creation of a "security-embedded hospitality" model, where the physical safety of the guest is integrated into the product offering without detracting from the experiential value. Only through this balance of high-tech security, proactive digital diplomacy, and high-touch hospitality can the industry continue to thrive as a primary driver of India’s economic future, effectively decoupling the reality of a modern, developing nation from the dated narratives of geopolitical instability. Ultimately, the future of Indian tourism lies in its ability to tell its own story—one of resilience, diversity, and an unwavering commitment to the visitor experience, regardless of the challenges it faces. The path forward is one of integration, innovation, and an unyielding commitment to the belief that the promise of exploration will always be greater than the fear of the unknown.

metadata.title: Colonial History on Animals' Bodies: A Study on the Shikar Writings of Lt.William Rice
metadata.description: Shikar literature is a vibrant but now ignored genre of literature among academic circles. Shikar literature deals with writings related to the act of hunting wild animals and exploration of jungles. The British administration in the nineteenth and early twentieth century India was interested not just in establishing its authority and control over the populace of India and its territories, it also aimed subtly to colonise the Indian jungles and the wild animals. Hunting and colonial policies on wildlife enabled the Colonisers to reshape their interactions with the natives of India and their compatriots- the wild animals. Hunting in the Indian wild was deeply implicated in the colonial state’s exercise of power after the first Indian War of Independence in 1857. Therefore, British policies on wildlife in the nineteenth catergorised wild animals as "vermins" and encouraged the white hunters to eradicate those vermins.  An attempt has been made in this paper to review the shikar writings of William Rice, one the earliest Shikar writers in Colonised India.

creators.name: Sharma V, Subrahmanya

metadata.title: Artificial Intelligence in Modern Marketing: Transforming Business Strategies and Customer Engagement
metadata.description:
Past few decades have witnessed a intense change in approach of businesses towards their customers. Artificial Intelligence (AI) is occupying the whole scenario across industries. Marketing - one of the most dynamic and consumer-oriented business functions - is going through a pronounced transformation.

AI technologies like machine learning, natural language processing, predictive analytics, chat bots,  and recommendation engines are influencing the decisions of organizations e.g. campaign designing, research in consumer behavior, fostering long-term customer relationships etc. 

Artificial Intelligence (AI) has emerged as a powerful support system in marketing. It is reshaping the business vividly, designing the business strategies and relationship management with customers. Advancement of digital technologies are used for data-driven decision making. Industries are slowly integrating AI tools like predictive analytics, machine learning, chat bots, for improving marketing effectiveness and consumer engagement.

This paper aims to study the role of AI in modern and advanced marketing and its effect on transforming business strategies and consumer engagement strategies by adopting descriptive and analytical approach. It is based on secondary data that has been derived from research articles, different authentic reports and books.  The findings point out that AI considerably improves the effectiveness of marketing strategies by applying targeted advertising, better customer perception, and enhanced customer relationship. AI facilitates high level of customer engagement, personalized recommendation for better customer experiences, instant customer support, and customized marketing communication.

The study concludes that the intentional adoption of AI can offer a competitive benefit in the vibrant digital market. It also emphasizes the necessity for organizations to invest in AI technologies for marvelous results. At the same time they should be ready to address challenges like data privacy, ethical concerns, and technological expertise.

journal.title: International Journal of Advance and Applied Research

metadata.title: Issues in Book Publishing in Sub-Saharan Africa
metadata.description:
Issues in Book Publishing in Sub-Saharan Africa brings together diverse perspectives on the challenges, opportunities, and emerging trends shaping the publishing industry across the African continent. The twenty chapters contained in this volume examine critical issues ranging from publishing for development, technological innovation, marketing, piracy, indigenous language publishing, children's literature, and the future of the book industry in Africa. Collectively, the chapters provide valuable insights for scholars, publishers, authors, policymakers, students, and other stakeholders interested in the advancement of African publishing.

Chapter One revisits the idea that book publishing can serve as a catalyst for socio-economic development. It clarifies key concepts such as development, book development, and publishing for development, while advocating a people-centred and transformational approach. The chapter emphasizes the need to align publishing activities with Africa's educational, cultural, and developmental goals.

Chapter Two examines the concept of Information and Communication Technology Integrated Books (ICTIB) in education. It explains how the incorporation of digital media, interactivity, and connectivity into textbooks can enhance teaching and learning. Drawing on studies conducted in Lagos, Nigeria, the chapter highlights the benefits of accessibility, personalization, and expanded educational reach.

Chapter Three explores the role of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in contemporary publishing. It introduces major AI tools used for idea generation, research, writing, and editing, and discusses their implications for authorship and publishing workflows. The chapter highlights both the opportunities and challenges associated with the increasing adoption of AI in content creation and dissemination.

Chapter Four presents a study of Nigerian university students' perceptions of textbooks. Using Social Responsibility Theory as a framework, the chapter reveals that students often have limited input in determining textbook quality. It identifies gaps in content relevance, language clarity, and book design, and recommends strategies for producing more learner-centred and sustainable educational publications.

Chapter Five traces the historical development of Nigeria's book market, with particular attention to the problem of piracy. It outlines the origins of publishing during the colonial era and examines the evolution of illegal book reproduction. The chapter also analyses how advances in digital and printing technologies have intensified piracy and discusses legal and institutional countermeasures adopted by publishers and policymakers.

Chapter Six investigates the transformative impact of digital technology on African publishing. It reviews global trends in digital publishing and marketing and assesses their relevance to African publishers. The chapter demonstrates how e-books, online sales platforms, and social media marketing are reshaping publishing business models and offers practical insights for industry adaptation.

Chapter Seven focuses on the challenges associated with the marketing and distribution of scholarly books in Africa. It notes that international markets often overlook locally produced scholarship and proposes strategies such as niche marketing, partnerships with academic networks, and the use of regional distribution channels. The chapter underscores the need for innovation in scholarly publishing and dissemination.

Chapter Eight provides a historical overview of book marketing initiatives in Africa. It highlights the establishment of key institutions such as national book councils, the African Books Collective (ABC), and the African Publishers Network (APNET). The chapter reviews major efforts aimed at improving book marketing and distribution while identifying persistent challenges, including low demand and inadequate funding.

Chapter Nine examines marketing strategies employed in Nigerian educational publishing. Through a survey of educational publishers, it analyses approaches such as institutional targeting, leveraging author reputation, and participation in trade fairs. The chapter also discusses the growing role of digital marketing and advocates stronger relationships between publishers and educational institutions.

Chapter Ten explores the history and impact of reprography and piracy in Nigeria. It traces the emergence of unauthorized reproduction practices and examines how technological developments have facilitated copyright infringement. The chapter highlights the negative consequences of piracy for authors and publishers and calls for stronger enforcement mechanisms and greater public awareness of copyright laws.

Chapter Eleven assesses the state of indigenous-language publishing in South Africa and Zambia. It reviews language policies and the linguistic diversity of both countries, revealing that indigenous-language publishing remains limited despite supportive policy frameworks. The chapter discusses challenges such as small markets and resource constraints while highlighting initiatives designed to promote local-language publishing.

Chapter Twelve traces the development of indigenous-language publishing in South Africa, from the apartheid era to the present day. It examines the historical suppression of African languages and the subsequent revival of indigenous literature in the post-apartheid period. The chapter identifies both opportunities and challenges in the sector and recommends measures to strengthen local-language publishing.

Chapter Thirteen investigates the decline of publishing in Kenya's indigenous languages. It presents evidence of the dominance of English-language publications and explores the difficulties faced by authors and publishers working in local languages. The chapter argues that economic and policy factors have contributed to linguistic marginalization and calls for deliberate efforts to preserve linguistic diversity through publishing.

Chapter Fourteen discusses strategies for cultivating a vibrant reading culture among children and young adults in Africa. It proposes innovative approaches to content development, emphasizing engaging storytelling, visual appeal, and thematic relevance. The chapter argues that reimagining literature for younger audiences is essential for fostering lifelong reading habits.

Chapter Fifteen reviews the current state of children's book publishing in Africa. It examines challenges such as limited readership, high production costs, and shortages of trained writers and illustrators. At the same time, the chapter highlights the cultural significance of children's literature and identifies emerging opportunities for growth and development across the continent.

Chapter Sixteen focuses on trends in writing and publishing for children in Kenya. It explores the historical foundations of children's literature, identifies recurring themes, and highlights the increasing contributions of Kenyan authors to the genre. The chapter demonstrates how local cultural experiences and contemporary social issues are reflected in children's books.

Chapter Seventeen analyses the publishing landscape in Cameroon. It identifies policy deficiencies, weak reading cultures, and limited institutional support as major obstacles to publishing development. The chapter argues for comprehensive national strategies and cultural interventions to strengthen knowledge dissemination and promote a sustainable book industry.

Chapter Eighteen examines the evolving relationship between authors and publishers in Africa. It considers the impact of digital technologies, open-access publishing, and institutional repositories on traditional publishing models. The chapter advocates collaborative approaches that can enhance knowledge production, dissemination, and access.

Chapter Nineteen surveys the broader information environment in Africa and its implications for publishing. It highlights factors such as dominant oral traditions, low literacy levels, and inadequate information infrastructure that continue to shape the publishing sector. The chapter concludes that investments in literacy development and information technology are essential for building a stronger publishing industry.

Chapter Twenty assesses the prospects and challenges of book publishing in Nigeria. It emphasizes the sector's importance to education, cultural development, and national growth while examining constraints such as inadequate infrastructure, limited funding, and persistent piracy. The chapter concludes by proposing policy interventions and strategic investments necessary for the sustainable advancement of the Nigerian publishing industry.

All together, the chapters in this volume provide a comprehensive examination of the contemporary publishing landscape in Sub-Saharan Africa. They illuminate the complexities of the industry while offering practical recommendations and future directions for strengthening book development, promoting literacy, preserving cultural heritage, and advancing knowledge production across the continent. The book is intended to serve as a valuable resource for scholars, publishers, authors, students, policymakers, and all stakeholders committed to the growth and transformation of African publishing.

metadata.title: Catching Sparks
metadata.description: Originally premiered at ZKM in Karlsruhe, Germany in 2023, Catching Sparks is a collaborative, interactive, improvisatory game-music installation. In Catching Sparks, up to three participants at a time can use small keyboard peripherals to catch sparks from a spinning wheel. These sparks, which come in different colors and respective sonic palettes, can be consumed to initiate a short (20- to 30-second) improvisation environment in which the player presses buttons to "perform" the spark. Sparks can also be passed to other players, and there are sonic and visual incentives to spreading the wealth if the wheel favors one player more than another. The ultimate goal, discovered on purpose or by accident, is to have three improvisors playing different sparks at the same time, which yields a vibrant visual and sonic display (however short-lived). This piece concretizes a metaphor for creative inspiration and its subsequent expression, a metaphor I often visualize as I compose. One must be in the right place and have the bandwidth to "catch" these bits of inspiration, and it is often more fruitful to collaborate in many expressive situations. With Catching Sparks, I hope to exhibit the playful nature of collaborative musicking in an exploratory paideic environment. To me, being together in community when making music is a panacea for modern fragmentation.

meeting.title: International Conference on New Interfaces for Musical Expression

metadata.title: Church Administration and Management in Nigeria: Insights from Emerging Scholars
metadata.description:
It is a profound privilege to have facilitated a course on Church Administration and Management at my university recently. One of my longstanding goals has been to empower postgraduate students not just with knowledge, but with the practical skils to write and publish academic papers that contribute meaningfully to their fields. To this end, I guided a group of enthusiastic students in transforming their term papers—submitted in partial fulfillment of the course requirements—into rigorous, publishable works. Under my direct supervision, these papers underwent thorough revisions, peer reviews, and refinements to meet scholarly standards.

This book represents a proud compilation of those efforts: a collection of selected papers authored by Nigerians, grounded in Nigerian contexts, and tailored specifically for church leaders across Nigeria and Africa. It stands as a deliberate act of decolonizing African thought. By encouraging African scholars to produce homegrown, contextually relevant scholarship—free from external impositions—Africans are reclaiming their narrative, address their unique ecclesiastical challenges, and foster intellectual independence. No longer must African church leaders rely solely on imported models. Here, indigenous voices apply time-tested principles of administration and management to the African vibrant faith communities.

The compendium features twelve (12) chapters, each crafted by some postgraduate students of the Department of Religious and Intercultural Studies, Lead City University (LCU), Ibadan, Nigeria who thoughtfully apply core theories and principles of church administration and management to their own denominations, churches, or ministries. These contributions span practical strategies for governance, financial stewardship, conflict resolution, leadership development, and community outreach, all viewed through an African lens.

Notably, nearly 75% of the chapter contributors are experiencing their first academic publication—a milestone that fills me with immense pride. The remaining 25% are emerging scholars with just one or two prior publications under their belts. Readers should approach this work with grace, recognizing it as the fruit of developing talents rather than flawless masterpieces. Perfection is elusive in any endeavour, after all. I extend my deepest appreciation to each contributor for their diligence: from the initial drafting and classroom presentations of their term papers, to the painstaking revisions that shaped these chapters. In a touch of authenticity, I encouraged them to include brief personal testimonies of their journeys—raw accounts of challenges overcome, breakthroughs achieved, and lessons learned. These narratives are more than footnotes; they are beacons of inspiration, whispering to other students and budding scholars: You can do this too!

As you turn these pages, I invite church leaders, administrators, and fellow academics to engage not just with the ideas, but with the spirit of innovation and resilience they embody. May this book spark transformation in your ministries and motivate a new generation of African Christian thinkers.

 

Rev. Adebayo Ola AFOLARANMI, PhD, JP, FSASS, MNASR

Department of Religious and Intercultural Studies,

Lead City University, Ibadan, Nigeria

metadata.title: The Role of Banaras in the Development of Hindu Sanatan culture : Insights from the Recent Years
metadata.description:
This paper examines the integral role of Banaras (Varanasi) in the development and preservation of Hindu Sanatan culture, particularly in recent years. As one of the oldest living cities in the world, Banaras serves as a vital nexus for religious, cultural, and philosophical traditions that define Sanatan Hinduism. This study employs a mixed-methods approach, combining historical analysis, ethnographic research, and contemporary case studies to explore the city's cultural dynamics. The methodology begins with a historical analysis of Banaras, tracing its evolution from ancient times to its current status as a cultural epicenter. Key texts, rituals, and festivals unique to Banaras are examined to illustrate their significance in shaping Sanatan cultural practices. Ethnographic methods, including interviews with local scholars, priests, and practitioners, provide firsthand insights into how traditional practices are maintained and adapted in the modern context.

Additionally, the paper investigates recent developments such as the revival of ancient arts, music, and rituals, alongside the role of educational institutions in promoting Sanatan culture. The impact of social media and technology in disseminating cultural knowledge is also analyzed, highlighting how these platforms foster global engagement with Banaras's rich heritage. Through this comprehensive approach, the study reveals how Banaras not only preserves the essence of Sanatan culture but also actively reinterprets it for contemporary society. The findings emphasize the city's ongoing relevance in the cultural landscape of Hinduism, illustrating its vital role in nurturing a living tradition that adapts to the changing needs of its community while remaining rooted in its historical and spiritual legacy.
keywords: Hindu Sanatan Culture, Ancient philosophy, Modern development in Banaras, Neo-Kashi.

metadata.title: Colonial Encounter and the Reassertion of the Indian Aesthetic Identity
metadata.description: This paper examines the profound impact of the British colonial encounter (c. 18th to mid-20th century) on the construction of Indian aesthetic identity and the subsequent, multifaceted processes of its reassertion. It argues that colonialism was not merely a political and economic domination but a fundamental epistemic violence that sought to dismantle, systematize, and re-orient indigenous aesthetic paradigms according to Western norms. The paper traces the trajectory from the initial devaluation and fossilization of Indian art forms to the complex nationalist responses, and finally to the postcolonial reclamation that characterizes the contemporary Indian art scene. Through an analysis of visual arts, literature, and institutional history, it demonstrates that the reassertion of Indian aesthetic identity is not a return to a pristine past, but a dynamic, often critical, engagement with both tradition and the colonial legacy, resulting in a vibrant, hybrid, and self-confident contemporary identity. Key theoretical frameworks from postcolonial studies, including the work of Edward Said, Homi Bhabha, and Gayatri Spivak, are employed to analyze this complex cultural negotiation.
keywords: Colonialism, Indian Aesthetics, Nationalism, Postcolonial Theory, Bengal School, Raja Ravi Varma, Swadeshi, Modern Indian Art, Cultural Identity, Hybridity.
references: Bhabha, H. K. (1994). The Location of Culture. Routledge. Coomaraswamy, A. K. (1934). The Transformation of Nature in Art. Harvard University Press. Dalmia, Y. (2001). When Was Modernism: Essays on Contemporary Cultural Practice in India. Tulika Books. Mathur, S. (2007). India by Design: Colonial History and Cultural Display. University of California Press.
